coredata.dc:description: The abuse of antibiotics leading to bacterial resistance has emerged as a significant impediment to wound healing. Consequently, this study developed antibacterial and anti-inflammatory bi-functional carbon dots (AAB-CDs) as the primary active material for the preparation of hydrogel wound dressings, aiming to promote wound healing. The synthesized AAB-CDs exhibited ultra-small size (2.3 nm), abundant surface functional groups, high Zeta potential (30.9 mV), and excellent biocompatibility. The positively charged AAB-CDs with outstanding antibacterial properties, mitigating the risk of bacterial resistance, while the abundant surface functional groups imparted stable antioxidant performance for sustained wound anti-inflammatory effects. Moreover, in vitro experiments demonstrated that the hydrogel dressings exhibited remarkable antibacterial properties against Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), and significantly promoted the proliferation of L929 fibroblasts. In vivo experiments further substantiated the feasibility of the hydrogel dressings in promoting wound healing and histological analysis revealed the biological mechanisms underlying the promotion of wound healing. In conclusion, AAB-CDs with robust antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties hold tremendous potential for promoting wound healing, providing practical insights for the biological application of CDs.

(f) 3D PL spectrum of AAB-CDs with excitation wavelength varying from 300 to 500 nm. (g) Zeta potential measurement of AAB-CDs. (g) Diagram of continuous release of AAB-CDs from EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel. (h) The CDs release of EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel at different pH values.

Simulation wound bed closure trace and wound contraction on the 3rd, 6th, 9th, and 12th days in the control, EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel with varying AAB-CDs concentrations (0 wt%, 1 wt%, 2 wt%, 3 wt%). (d) Relative wound area calculated based on corresponding digital images; (e) Scar area on day 12. Data are shown as mean values ± SD. Bars represent standard error, n = 7 per group.

(a) Schematic diagram of wound healing process. (b) Representative H&E stained histologic longitudinal sections of different groups. The scale bars are 1 mm. (c) The immunostaining images of CD31 (indicated with purple arrows). The scale bars are 0.1 mm. (d) Neovascularization in different groups. The double staining revealed that CD31(+) structures were surrounded by α-SMA positive cells (indicated with yellow arrows). The scale bars are 0.1 mm. (e) Masson's trichrome staining histological longitudinal sections for collagen deposition in granulation tissues after different treatments at day 12 to evaluate inflammation and collagen deposition. The scale bars are 0.1 mm. (f) statistical analysis of the relative connective tissue thickness in the five groups. (g) Quantification of the CD31 labeled structures. (h) Quantification of collagen fiber rate. Data are presented as mean with standard deviation as error bars, n = 7, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01.

head.ce:title: Antibacterial and anti-inflammatory Bi-functional carbon dots hydrogel dressing for robust promotion of wound healing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The abuse of antibiotics leading to bacterial resistance has emerged as a significant impediment to wound healing. Consequently, this study developed antibacterial and anti-inflammatory bi-functional carbon dots (AAB-CDs) as the primary active material for the preparation of hydrogel wound dressings, aiming to promote wound healing. The synthesized AAB-CDs exhibited ultra-small size (2.3 nm), abundant surface functional groups, high Zeta potential (30.9 mV), and excellent biocompatibility. The positively charged AAB-CDs with outstanding antibacterial properties, mitigating the risk of bacterial resistance, while the abundant surface functional groups imparted stable antioxidant performance for sustained wound anti-inflammatory effects. Moreover, in vitro experiments demonstrated that the hydrogel dressings exhibited remarkable antibacterial properties against Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), and significantly promoted the proliferation of L929 fibroblasts. In vivo experiments further substantiated the feasibility of the hydrogel dressings in promoting wound healing and histological analysis revealed the biological mechanisms underlying the promotion of wound healing. In conclusion, AAB-CDs with robust antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties hold tremendous potential for promoting wound healing, providing practical insights for the biological application of CDs.

Robust antibacterial and anti-inflammatory bi-functional carbon dots (AAB-CDs) are designed with ultra-small size (2.3 nm), high Zeta potential (30.9 mV) and remarkable antioxidant ability. Then AAB-CDs are doped in biocompatible hydrogel dressing fabricated with the monomers of N-vinyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NVP) and N-(hydroxymethyl) acrylamide (NMA). The resultant hydrogel dressing exhibits continuous AAB-CDs release capacity for long-acting treatment that promotes wound healing.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a type of quantum dot (QD), have more abundant surface functionalization compared to semiconductor QDs and perovskite QDs. Besides the excellent fluorescence properties, CDs also possess various physicochemical properties including tunable oxidability, reducibility, and surface charge [1–4]. This makes CDs widely applicable in the field of biological marking, wound healing promotion, and cancer treatment [5–7]. CDs naturally possess good biocompatibility and low toxicity, making them increasingly application in recent years as antibacterial and anti-inflammatory nanomaterials for promoting wound healing.

The skin is the largest organ of the human body, protecting the body from bacterial infections and sensing external stimuli [8]. However, the skin is fragile and prone to damage. The process of skin healing can be prolonged by bacterial infections and resultant inflammation [9]. The abuse of antibiotics in clinical settings has led to bacterial resistance, making the development of non-antibiotic antimicrobial agents particularly important [10,11]. Against this backdrop, developing CD-based wound dressings has become an effective method for promoting wound healing in recent years. Chen et al. prepared positively charged CDs using polyethyleneimine (PEI) and p-phenylenediamine through a hydrothermal method. Spraying these CDs on the superficial wounds of mice effectively cleared Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), alleviated excessive inflammatory responses, and thus promoted wound healing [12]. Zhu et al. used ginger as a raw material to prepare anti-inflammatory CDs via microwave method and focused on studying the anti-inflammatory mechanisms of ginger CDs [13]. Shan et al. synthesized near-infrared luminescent CDs with spectral antibacterial properties for photodynamic therapy. These CDs interact with hydrogen peroxide in the tumor cell environment to produce chemiluminescence, generating reactive oxygen species in situ for killing pathogens [14]. Puvvada et al. synthesized positively charged CDs that promote wound healing using 8-hydroxy julolidine and citric acid as raw materials through the microwave method. The resultant CDs can induce macrophages to transition from the pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype to the anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype by inhibiting the expression of CD80, thus reducing the duration of wound inflammation and promoting healing [9]. In reported cases, CDs that promote wound healing mainly exhibit reductive or positive charge characteristics. Positively charged CDs can kill bacteria through electrostatic interactions and prevent bacterial resistance. The reductive properties of CDs can be used to clear excess reactive oxygen species in wounds, thereby reducing inflammation and speeding up wound healing.

In this work, we selected ascorbic acid and PEI as precursors to prepared antibacterial and anti-inflammatory bifunctional carbon dots (AAB-CDs) via hydrothermal method (Fig. 1 a). The results showed that AAB-CDs have an ultra-small average diameter (2.3 nm), high Zeta potential (30.9 mV) and good antioxidant properties. We then doped AAB-CDs and ε-poly(l-lysine) (EPL) in hydrogel prepared with monomers of N-vinyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NVP) and N-(hydroxymethyl) acrylamide (NMA) to prepare CDs-based hydrogel (EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs) wound dressings capable of continuously releasing CDs to realize the promotion of wound healing (Fig. 1b and c). The in vitro experiments demonstrated that EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel dressings exhibited remarkable antibacterial properties against Escherichia coli (E. coli) and S. aureus, and significantly promoted the proliferation of L929 fibroblasts. In vivo experiments further substantiated the feasibility of the hydrogel dressings in promoting wound healing and histological analysis revealed the biological mechanisms underlying the promotion of wound healing.

Ascorbic acid (99 %), salicylic acid (99 %), ferrous sulfate (FeSO4, 98 %), polyethyleneimine (PEI, Mw = 1800) and 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT, 98 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. ε-Poly-l-lysine (EPL, Mw < 5000), N-(hydroxymethyl) acrylamide (NMA, 98 %), N,N′-methylene diacrylamide (MBAA, 99 %), N-vinyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NVP, 99 %) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. 2-Hydroxy-4'-(2-hydroxyethoxy)-2-methylpropiophenone (Photoinitiator 2959, 98 %) was purchased from Shanghai Houcheng Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Chloral hydrate solution (5 % w/v) was purchased from Beijing Bai Ao Lai Bo Technology Co., Ltd. Deionized water with the resistivity greater than 18 MΩ cm−1 was used in the experiments. L929 fibroblasts were obtained from the Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China) and cultured in 89 % dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM, Gibco, USA) supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, Gibco, USA) and 1 % Penicillin-Streptomycin, Liquid (Gibco, USA).

AAB-CDs were synthesized via one-pot hydrothermal method. In a typical experiment process, 1.0 g of ascorbic acid and 10.0 g of PEI were completely dissolved in 20 mL water. Then the mixed solution was transferred to a 50 mL hydrothermal reactor and reacted at 180 °C for 6 h. After the reaction completed, the obtained crude AAB-CDs were preliminarily purified by a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Then the obtained AAB-CDs were further purified by dialysis treatment with a 500 Da dialysis dag. Finally, the AAB-CDs powders were obtained by lyophilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of antioxidant capacity of AAB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The evaluation of antioxidant capacity of AAB-CDs was performed by determination the scavenging capacities of 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) free radicals, and •OH.

Determination of DPPH. The method for determination of DPPH was reported in previous work. In a typical experiment, 900 μL of 100 μM DPPH-ethanol solution was added to 100 μL of AAB-CDs solution with different concentrations. Then the mixed solution was incubated for 5 h in the dark at 25 °C. The absorption of the resultant solution at 517 nm was determined to assess the rate of AAB-CDs on DPPH scavenging capacity, which was calculated using following equation:
DPPH
scavenging
capacity
( % )
=
A a
－
A
s
A a
× 100 %
A a and A s represent the absorbance of the blank and sample, respectively.

Determination of •OH. The determination of •OH was carried out by Fenton reaction. In a typical experiment, 400 μL of mixed water solution containing 7.5 mM FeSO4 and 1.8 mM salicylic acid was added to 100 μL of AAB-CDs solution with different concentrations, followed by the addition of 100 μL of 0.2 M H2O2 into the resultant solution. Then the mixed solution was incubated for 15 min at 37 °C. The absorption of the resultant solution at 510 nm was determined to assess the rate of AAB-CDs on •OH scavenging capacity, which was calculated using following equation:

A b and A s stand for the absorbance of the blank and sample, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA) and EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, 10 g of NVP, 1 g of NMA, 0.48 g of EPL, 0.05 g of MBAA, and 0.1 g of photoinitiator 2959 were dissolved in 1 g of water. This mixture was then transferred to a 25 mL beaker and subjected to vigorous stirring, thus forming the precursor solution for the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA) hydrogel. Subsequently, the precursor solution was aliquoted into 5 mL petri dishes, followed by exposure to ultraviolet radiation for a duration of 10 s. This irradiation facilitates the free radical polymerization of NVP and NMA in the presence of EPL, resulting in the formation of the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA) hydrogel. In a parallel approach, EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogels with varying concentrations of AAB-CDs were synthesized. Different mass concentrations of AAB-CDs (1 wt%, 2 wt%, and 3 wt%) with a fixed concentration of 1 mg/mL are incorporated into the precursor solution. The resulting AAB-CDs-incorporated composite hydrogels are designated as EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs (1 wt%), EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs (2 wt%), and EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs (3 wt%), corresponding to their respective AAB-CDs concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Swelling performance test of EPL@Poly(NVP-co-NMA) and EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para:
For the swelling test, hydrogel samples of dimensions 2 × 2 cm2 were prepared (five samples of each). These samples were then submerged in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). Subsequent to designated time intervals, the samples were carefully extracted from the PBS. Excess PBS on the surface of hydrogels was carefully dried by filter paper, followed by an accurate determination of swollen samples' mass. The swelling ratio is quantified utilizing the formula:
Swelling
Ratio
( % )
=
W 1
－
W 0
W 0
× 100 %
W 1 denotes the mass of the hydrogel following swelling, while W 0 represents the initial mass of the hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro study of the sustained release characteristics of AAB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The EPL@Poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs-3 hydrogel with a 3 wt% concentration of AAB-CDs was soaked in 5 mL of PBS solution (containing 10 % ethanol) with varying pH values (5.5, 6.5, and 7.5), and continuously agitated at 37 °C. At predetermined time intervals, 200 μL of the release medium were extracted for release profile analysis and replenished with an equivalent volume of fresh medium solution. The quantity of released AAB-CDs was measured using a microplate reader with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 427 nm. The percentage release of AAB-CDs was calculated using the following equation:
AAB ‐ CDs
Release
Rate
( % )
=
M t
M 0
× 100 %
M t represents the cumulative amount of AAB-CDs released at time “t”, and M 0 indicates the initial quantity of AAB-CDs encapsulated in the EPL@Poly(NVP-co-NMA)-AAB-CDs-3 hydrogel.

Initially, Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 25923) and Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922), obtained from the National Center for Clinical Laboratories, were selected as model pathogens for antimicrobial activity experiments. Before conducting the experiments, S. aureus and E. coli were cultured for 24 h at 37 °C in LB nutrient agar to determine its concentration. Subsequently, hydrogels with a diameter of 0.5 cm and a thickness of 3 mm, containing varying concentrations of AAB-CDs, were prepared and transferred to LB agar plates coated with 100 μL 106 CFU/mL fresh suspensions of S. aureus and E. coli. After overnight incubation at 37 °C, the bacterial inhibition area around the hydrogel disc was measured. Each experiment was repeated five times.

i) Initially, ultra-low attachment 24-well plates were sterilized using the ultraviolet sterilization method for 30 min. Subsequently, the pure hydrogel and composite hydrogels containing three different concentrations of AAB-CDs were placed into these sterilized plates. Differentiated L929 fibroblasts were then introduced, along with 0.5 mL of DMEM medium (containing 10 % NBS, 1 % Penicillin-Streptomycin). The samples were incubated at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO2. ii) In a similar manner, the four hydrogel samples were placed into 24-well plates that have undergone ultraviolet sterilization. 40000 L929 fibroblasts were seeded per well, followed by the addition of 0.5 mL of DMEM medium (containing 10 % NBS, 1 % Penicillin-Streptomycin). These samples were also cultured at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere enriched with 5 % CO2. All experiments were conducted in triplicate. The viability of cells in both the pure hydrogel and the composite hydrogels with varying concentrations of AAB-CDs was investigated using live/dead cell staining techniques. At the 72-h mark, adipocytes were stained with an Actin antibody (abcam 179467), while L929 fibroblasts were stained with calcein AM and propidium iodide. Finally, the survival of cells within the hydrogels was observed under a Leica fluorescence microscope.

To assess the biocompatibility of the hydrogel dressing, an MTT assay was performed to calculate the survival rate of L929 mouse fibroblasts. First, the hydrogel extract was used to culture the cells, replacing the traditional DMEM medium. Subsequently, after 24 or 48 h of co-culture, MTT was added to each well and incubated for 2 h. By recording the absorbance at 490 nm, the survival rate of the cells was determined. Survival rates were calculated relative to control cells.

Initially, Sprague-Dawley rats, aged 8 weeks and weighing between 220 and 260 g, were anesthetized via intraperitoneal injection with chloral hydrate solution (0.7 mL/100 g body weight). The dorsal fur of the rats was meticulously shaved. On each rat's back, a 1.5 cm × 1.5 cm full-thickness cutaneous wound was surgically created. The rats were then randomized into five distinct groups. The wounds were infected with S. aureus and E. coli, followed by a 48 h incubation period to establish the infection. The experimental setup includes a control group, which receives no intervention, and four treatment groups, each administered with different hydrogel formulations: EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs (0 wt%), EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs (1 wt%), EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs (2 wt%), and EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs (3 wt%) hydrogels, respectively. These hydrogels, precisely cut to 1.5 cm × 1.5 cm, were directly applied onto the wounds owing to their superior adhesive characteristics. The rats were subsequently housed in individual cages and received daily care. Wound healing process was meticulously monitored and documented on days 0, 3, 6, 9, and 12. Upon the wounds were fully healed, granulation tissues from each group were excised for analysis. For comprehensive examination, each sample was divided into two parts: one section was immersed in neutral formalin for histological and immunohistochemical evaluations, while the other was cryopreserved in liquid nitrogen for subsequent immunofluorescence staining. For statistical purposes, n = 7 per group.

The granulation tissue samples were extracted from neutral formaldehyde, subjected to dehydration, and embedded in paraffin. Subsequently, 5 μm thick tissue sections were obtained using a microtome for histological and immunohistochemical investigations. For hematoxylin and eosin (H & E) staining, the cell nuclei were stained bright blue with hematoxylin, while the cytoplasm was stained with eosin ranging from various shades of pink to peach red, and acidophilic granules in the cytoplasm appeared intensely bright red. Masson's trichrome staining revealed that cell nuclei, collagen, and fibers were colored blue, while cytoplasm, muscle, and red blood cells were colored red. For neovascularization assessment, tissue sections were incubated overnight at 4 °C with primary antibodies CD31 (abcam, ab182981) and α-smooth muscle actin (α-SMA, Proteintech). Here, red represents CD31, and blue represents α-SMA.

A cohort of 35 robust male Sprague-Dawley rats, procured from Nanjing Medical University in China and weighing between 220 and 260 g, participated in this study. All animal research was conducted in accordance with and obtained approval from the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC-20220314-02) of the Jiangsu Center for Safety Evaluation of Drugs, adhering to the principles of experimental animal welfare and all applicable national and local laws.

The experimental data were subjected to a comprehensive statistical analysis, conducted in triplicate, and are presented as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). Unpaired Student's t-test or one-way analysis of variance was employed for the statistical analysis. Significance was determined at a threshold of p < 0.05, where *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001 denoted varying levels of significance. Graphs and statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism software version 7 and OriginPro 2020 (Learning Edition). Histological examination included the capture of a minimum of five fields per section, and subsequent image analysis was carried out using Image J software.

The morphology, structure of AAB-CDs and hydrogel dressings were measured by JEOL JEM 2100plus transmission electron microscope (TEM) and HITACHI S-4800 scanning electron microscope (SEM), respectively. The composition of AAB-CDs was measured by Rigaku X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD), ES-CAIAB250 X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer. PL curves were recorded on a Lengguang F97 Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a Shimadzu UV-1900i spectrophotometer. Time-resolved PL decay curve and absolute PL QYs were measured on an Edinburgh FLS 980 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Mechanical properties of hydrogels were tested on Shanghai SNSS (C42.104) universal testing machine.

In CDs-based hydrogel wound dressings, the performance of CDs is a key factor affecting the curative effect of wound healing. The morphology of the AAB-CDs was recorded by Transmission electron microscope (TEM). The TEM image of the AAB-CDs (Fig. 2 a) shows that AAB-CDs are uniformly dispersed without any aggregation, which is beneficial for their stable and continuous release from the hydrogel matrix. The High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of AAB-CDs (Fig. S1a) displays clear lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.18 nm (Fig. S1b), corresponding to the (102) plane of graphite carbon [15]. The size distribution of AAB-CDs was shown in Fig. S1c, displaying an average diameter of 2.3 nm. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum of AAB-CDs (Fig. 2b) shows a broad peak at 2θ = 19.5°, corresponding to the turbostratic carbon phase or single crystal graphite [16]. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum of the AAB-CDs (Fig. 2c) reveals that the peak located at 3270 cm−1 is regarded as the bending vibrations of O–H and N–H [17], the peak at 2930 cm−1 is regarded as the bending vibration of –CH3 [14], the peaks at 2830 cm−1 and 1346 cm−1 are attributed to the bending vibrations of C–H [18,19], the peak at 1620 cm−1 is regarded as the bending vibration of CN [20], the peak at 1140 cm−1 is regarded as the bending vibration of C–O [21], the peaks at 1445 and 1304 cm−1 are attributed to the bending vibrations of C–N [21,22]. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectrum (Fig. 2d) shows that AAB-CDs contain C, N, and O elements with atomic ratios of 70.67 %, 12.53 %, and 16.80 %, respectively. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. S2a) confirms the main bonding states of C element are regarded as C–N (285.14 eV) [23], C–O (287.03 eV) [24] and C–C (284.05 eV) [25,26]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. S2b) shows that the N element in AAB-CDs is mainly present as Pyridine N (398.37 eV) [27,28], Tertiary N (399.15 eV) [29], Pyrrolic N (399.6 eV) [30] and –NO3 - (405.72 eV) [31]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. S2c) reveals that the oxygen is mainly in the form of CO (531.69 eV) [32] and O–H (530.26 eV) [33]. The optical properties of AAB-CDs were analysized to understand the structure-property relationship. The emission and excitation spectra of AAB-CDs (Fig. 2e) show a photoluminescent (PL) emission peak at 532 nm and an excitation peak at 438 nm, indicating the optimal excitation wavelength for AAB-CDs is 438 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of AAB-CDs (Fig. S3a) shows a significant absorption peak at 338 nm, corresponding to the absorption of COO− groups [34]. The PL quantum yield (QY) spectrum of AAB-CDs (Fig. S3b), with deionized water as the reference, reveals an absolute PL QY of 10 %. Additionally, we observed the excitation-dependent characteristics of AAB-CDs, a typical fluorescent emission behavior of CDs. Fig. 2f displays the PL emission wavelength of AAB-CDs ranges from 480 to 560 nm when excited at wavelengths between 400 and 500 nm. The PL lifetime decay and fitting curve of AAB-CDs (Fig. S3c) show a bi-exponential decay with an average PL lifetime of 4.8 ns. It is particularly important to test the surface charge and reductive properties of AAB-CDs with antibacterial and antioxidative characteristics. The Zeta potential test of AAB-CDs (Fig. 2g) shows Zeta potential of 30.9 mV. The antioxidant capacity of AAB-CDs was evaluated by using AAB-CDs to scavenging 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) free radicals and •OH [12]. The absorption spectrum of DPPH ethanol solution in the presence of different concentrations of AAB-CDs (Fig. 2h) shows that the absorption intensity at 517 nm gradually decreases with increasing the concentration of AAB-CDs. The DPPH scavenging capacity under different AAB-CDs concentrations (Fig. S4a) shows a near-linear change, indicating the stable antioxidant capacity of AAB-CDs. When the concentration of AAB-CDs reachs 200 μg/mL, the DPPH scavenging capacity is about 87.3 %. Fig. 2i shows the absorption spectrum of the mixture solution in the presence of different concentrations of AAB-CDs to determinate •OH, exhibiting that absorption intensity at 510 nm decreases gradually with the increase of AAB-CDs concentration. Fig. S4b shows that the relationship between AAB-CDs concentration and scavenging rate of •OH is close to a straight line, indicating the high stability of AAB-CDs in scavenging •OH. When the AAB-CDs concentration is 200 μg/mL, the scavenging rate of •OH can reach 76.9 %. In summary, AAB-CDs have an ultra-small diameter, uniform size, and homogeneous distribution. The PL performance of AAB-CDs is stable, having the potential for fluorescence imaging. Additionally, the positive charge and reductive properties of AAB-CDs endow them with robust antibacterial and antioxidative capabilities, making them widely applicable in promoting wound healing.

In order to facilitate the application of AAB-CDs for wound healing, hydrogels are selected as the matrix for loading AAB-CDs to construct wound dressings. NVP and NMA were used as monomers to prepare hydrogel materials through photo-initiated polymerization. During the preparation process, EPL and AAB-CDs were introduced, resulting in the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel wound dressings. EPL exhibits good biocompatibility and can synergistically enhance the antibacterial properties when combined with AAB-CDs. Additionally, the hydrogen bonding between EPL and the poly(NVP-co-NMA) hydrogel forms a double network structure, enhancing the mechanical strength of the hydrogel. During the clinical application of EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel wound dressings, AAB-CDs are continuously and stably released from the hydrogel, facilitating sustained antibacterial and anti-inflammatory effects, thereby promoting wound healing. Fig. 3 a and b shows the SEM images of the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA) and EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogels, respectively. Both hydrogels exhibit a porous structure, with a noticeable reduction in pore size upon the introduction of AAB-CDs. Fig. S6 shows the TEM image of EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel, showcasing the homogeneous dispersion of AAB-CDs within the hydrogel matrix. The red dashed circles demarcate the localization of AAB-CDs, discernible by the pronounced lattice fringes within the delineated regions. Fig. 3c presents the FTIR spectra of the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA) and EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogels. Apart from the intensity of the peaks, no new peaks appeared, indicating that the introduction of AAB-CDs did not change the structure or chemical properties of the hydrogel. The peak at 3283 cm−1 corresponds to the –OH stretching vibration in EPL and NMA [35], while the peaks at 1653 cm−1 and 1539 cm−1 are attributed to the Amide I and Amide II in EPL and NMA [36]. The peak at 1276 cm−1 is due to the C–O in the hydrogel [37]. The water swelling kinetics of the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA) and EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogels were researched in-depth using a gravimetric analysis method [38]. Fig. S5 shows the comparison pictures of the two hydrogels before and after swelling. Fig. 3d demonstrates that both hydrogels reached swelling equilibrium after 10 h, with swelling ratios of 249 % and 131 % for the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA) and EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogels, respectively. The introduction of AAB-CDs reduced the concentration of free radicals, leading to longer kinetic chains, which ultimately resulted in smaller pore size and a lower swelling ratio in the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel [38]. Fig. 3e and f compare the mechanical strength of the hydrogels with different concentrations of AAB-CDs. As the concentration of AAB-CDs increased, both the tensile and compressive strengths of the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel gradually increased, due to the formation of hydrogen bonds and covalent bonds between AAB-CDs and the hydrogel, significantly enhancing the mechanical properties of the hydrogel [39–43]. A major factor affecting the wound healing performance of the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel dressing is the in vitro sustained release capability of AAB-CDs within the hydrogel. Fig. 3g illustrates the schematic of AAB-CDs being slowly released from the hydrogel. Fig. 3h shows the release rate of AAB-CDs from the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel in PBS buffer solutions at pH values of 5.5, 6.5, and 7.5 with different times. It is observed that over 1–10 h of the processes, the release rate of AAB-CDs gradually increased in all three pH environments, with a trend close to linear, indicating that the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel has a stable and sustained CD release capability, which is beneficial in promoting wound healing.

The antibacterial property of wound dressings is paramount, given the potential for bacterial infections to impede the speed of wound healing and exacerbate local conditions [44,45]. To investigate the antibacterial effects of EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel, E. coli (Gram-negative) and S. aureus (Gram-positive) were selected as model bacteria. In Fig. 4 a and b, bacterial concentrations were assessed by measuring absorbance values, revealing that the experimental groups (EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogels with different AAB-CDs concentrations) exhibited more remarkable inhibition against S. aureus and E. coli compared to the filter paper (control group). Particularly noteworthy is the reduction in optical density values of EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel with increasing AAB-CDs concentrations, indicating a progressively significant antibacterial effect, especially against S. aureus. For E. coli, similar antibacterial effects were observed at 2 wt% and 3 wt% AAB-CDs concentrations. Additionally, the inhibition zones of EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogels with different AAB-CDs concentrations (1 wt%, 2 wt%, 3 wt%) were measured. The results indicated minimal inhibitory effects of pure hydrogel on bacterial spread, while 2 wt% and 3 wt% AAB-CDs concentrations of EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs showed significant inhibitory zones, effectively suppressing the growth of both bacterial strains (Fig. 4c). The recorded inhibition zone diameters were as follows: for S. aureus, EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogels with different AAB-CDs concentrations (1 wt%, 2 wt%, 3 wt%) were 10 mm, 12.3 mm, 28.54 mm, and 22.4 mm, respectively; for E. coli, they were 11.25 mm, 15.2 mm, 23.75 mm, and 26.22 mm (Fig. S7). Moreover, we compared the antibacterial properties of hydrogels containing EPL and those without EPL through inhibition zone experiments. The results indicate that gels containing EPL exhibit significantly better antibacterial effects compared to those without EPL. This conclusion is further supported by the synergistic enhancement of antibacterial performance in hydrogels through the combined use of EPL and AAB-CDs (Fig. S8). To further elucidate the antibacterial mechanism of the hydrogel dressings, we hypothesis revolves around the pivotal role played by the release of positively charged AAB-CDs from EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs within the aqueous solution, alongside the inherent biopolymer EPL carried by the hydrogel itself. On one hand, the liberation of these AAB-CDs by the hydrogel results in their adhesion onto bacterial surfaces, culminating in the formation of intricate polymeric films. These films impede the transfer of nutrients, disrupt normal bacterial metabolic processes, and induce damage to bacterial membranes, as depicted in Fig. 4d [12,46,47]. On the other hand, EPL, recognized as a naturally occurring cationic polypeptide and an FDA-approved safe polymer in the United States, demonstrates outstanding biocompatibility and antibacterial efficacy, inclusive of addressing multidrug resistance (MDR) concerns [48]. Furthermore, the antibacterial activity attributed to EPL operates through its modulation of bacterial cell surface architecture and physiological metabolism [49]. Importantly, by directly exposing the hydrogel to microorganisms, SEM images of the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel reveal alterations in the roughness and morphology of bacterial cells, further validating the antibacterial mechanism of the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel (Fig. S9). These findings suggest that EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel exhibits significant antibacterial potential, effectively inhibiting the proliferation of E. coli and S. aureus, offering promise for preventing secondary infections.

The biocompatibility of EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel was evaluated through the 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay and cell migration [46,50]. EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogels fabricated with different AAB-CDs concentrations were co-cultured with cell culture medium for 24 h at 37 °C, and then culture medium was obtained for the culture of L929 fibroblasts and NIH-3T3 cells. As shown in Fig. S10a, after 24 h of cultivation, the L929 fibroblasts viability rates for the control group and EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel groups with AAB-CDs concentrations of 0 wt%, 1 wt%, 2 wt%, and 3 wt% were 37.425 ± 0.586 %, 60.665 ± 4.29 %, 63.27 ± 1.56 %, 59.91 ± 3.19 %, and 69.25 ± 0.29 %, respectively. After 48 h of cultivation, the viability rates for each group were 37.275 ± 7.6 %, 70.94 ± 0.9 %, 79.67 ± 6.06 %, 80.36 ± 2.5 %, and 99.09 ± 3.87 %, respectively. Similarly, following 24 and 48 h of cultivation, there was a significant increase in the viability of NIH-3T3 cells with the escalation of AAB-CDs concentrations, along with evident observations of cellular proliferation (Fig. S10b). Moreover, the results of cell migration experiments indicate that after 24 h, both the control group and the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel groups with AAB-CDs concentrations of 0 wt%, 1 wt%, 2 wt%, and 3 wt% demonstrated an increase in cell viability with the escalation of AAB-CDs concentration. Furthermore, significant differences were observed among the five groups after 48 h, particularly notable in the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel group with an AAB-CDs concentration of 3 wt%, where the number of surviving cells exceeded 100 % (Fig. S11). Thus, EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogels promote the proliferation and migration of L929 fibroblasts and NIH-3T3 cells, suggesting its potential significance in the wound healing process.

EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel exhibits significant potential for treating S. aureus- and E. coli-infected skin defects due to its outstanding antibacterial and antioxidant properties. The therapeutic strategy, as illustrated in Fig. 5 a, was further assessed in a rat model to evaluate the therapeutic effects of EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogels on infected wounds. Initially, a full-thickness skin defect model (1.5 cm × 1.5 cm) was created on the dorsum of rats, followed by the induction of infectious wounds through S. aureus and E. coli infection. Subsequently, the rats with infected wounds were divided into five groups (control group and four experimental groups with varying AAB-CDs concentrations of EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogels). Wound healing progress was monitored with photographs, and wound area was calculated to assess the healing process (Fig. 5b). Additionally, the closure trajectory of the wound bed and wound contraction were simulated to visually highlight the characteristics of the healing process (Fig. 5c). The results of the wound healing experiment showed that on the third day, the wound areas in all four experimental groups were comparable to the control group, but the experimental groups no longer exhibited wound exudation, indicating a significant advantage over the control group. By the sixth day, there was no wound exudation in any of the groups. On the ninth day, the wound areas in all groups had decreased compared to the initial measurements. Notably, the wounds in the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs (3 wt%) group were close to closure and exhibited a significant advantage over the control group and EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs (0 wt%, 1 wt%, 2 wt%) groups. After 12 days of treatment, wounds treated with EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs (3 wt%) hydrogel showed almost no open wounds, presenting smooth neoepithelial tissue. In contrast, the control group and EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs (0 wt%, 1 wt%, 2 wt%) groups still had open wounds with percentages of 12.25 ± 4.56 %, 10.39 ± 4.25 %, 9.25 ± 3.56 %, and 8.31 ± 3.65 %, respectively (Fig. 5d). These findings strongly support the significant promoting effect of EPL@poly(NVP-co- NMA)@AAB-CDs (3 wt%) on wound healing, attributed to the antibacterial and antioxidant properties of AAB-CDs. Additionally, scar area was measured on the 12th day, revealing that the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@CDs (3 wt%) group had the smallest scar area, demonstrating excellent skin regeneration capability (Fig. 5e). Therefore, our study reveals the immense potential value of EPL@poly(NVP-co- NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel in treating infectious wounds, providing robust support and profound insights for the improvement of future wound healing methods. This innovative research not only injects new perspectives into the field of wound healing but also offers valuable guidance for the development and application of similar materials.

To elucidate the biological mechanisms during the healing process, we systematically studied regenerative granulation tissue, collagen fibers at the wound site, and neoangiogenesis through hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, Masson staining, and immunofluorescence staining. Fig. 6 a illustrates the formation of skin tissue and the wound healing process. Initially, tissue slices from 12 days of healing were evaluated for the growth of regenerative granulation through H&E staining, followed by quantitative analysis (Fig. 6b and f). H&E fluorescence staining images revealed that the thickness of granulation tissue in the control group and EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs (0 wt%, 1 wt%, 2 wt%, 3 wt%) was 0.98 ± 0.14, 1.125 ± 0.145, 1.383 ± 0.168, 1.62 ± 0.26, and 1.83 ± 0.278 mm, respectively, highlighting the optimal granulation growth in the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs (3 wt%) group. Compared to other groups, the control group exhibited poorer wound healing, indicating the lack of additional protective measures. Moreover, vascular formation holds paramount significance in tissue development and maturation [8,51]. Therefore, CD31 immunofluorescence staining was employed, and quantitative analysis was conducted to assess vascular formation at the wound site (Fig. 6c). Wounds treated with EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogels in the experimental groups showed a significant increase in vascular density with the increasing AAB-CDs concentration, especially in the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs (3 wt%) group. Additionally, platelet endothelial cell adhesion molecule-1 (PECAM-1/CD31) serves as a marker for endothelial cells, while α-smooth muscle actin (α-SMA) marks vascular smooth muscle cells [52,53]. The spatial relationship between CD31 and α-SMA staining indicates the formation of mature blood vessels. Consequently, the vascular structure in regenerating tissue was further assessed through CD31 and α-SMA immunostaining. Fig. 6d presents representative images of the vascular structure at the wound site for each group. The vascular structure in the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogels group was denser, while the vascular structure in the control group was relatively sparse. In the control group and EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs (0 wt%, 1 wt%, 2 wt%, 3 wt%), the vascular structure generation was 18.3 ± 1.46, 20.45 ± 3.98, 45.93 ± 4.78, 62.659 ± 5.86, and 65.689 ± 6.34 mm, respectively (Fig. 6g). These results clearly demonstrate the significant effect of EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs in promoting neoangiogenesis. Furthermore, Masson staining revealed the highest collagen deposition in the EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs (3 wt%) hydrogel group, followed by the groups of hydrogels with 2 wt% and 1 wt% AAB-CDs, while the control group exhibited the lowest collagen deposition (Fig. 6e and h). Therefore, EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs hydrogel demonstrates superior performance in wound healing applications. Overall, EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs exhibits significant advantages and clinical importance in wound healing, wound protection, and tissue regeneration, particularly in epithelial regeneration, neoangiogenesis, and collagen arrangement.

In this study, we employed a hydrothermal method to synthesize AAB-CDs with antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties. The resultant AAB-CDs were utilized as the primary active material for the preparation of hydrogel wound dressings aimed at promoting wound healing. The positively charged AAB-CDs demonstrated remarkable antibacterial capabilities, and their antioxidant properties effectively suppressed wound inflammation, showcasing outstanding potential in promoting wound healing. The abundant functional groups on the surface of AAB-CDs interacted with the hydrogel matrix through forces such as hydrogen bonding, significantly enhancing the mechanical properties of the hydrogel dressing. This facilitated the stable release of AAB-CDs, thereby achieving prolonged enhancement of wound healing performance. Furthermore, in vitro experiments robustly validated the excellent biocompatibility of AAB-CDs, along with their antibacterial properties against E. coli and S. aureus. In vivo experiments substantiated the exceptional wound healing promotion characteristics of AAB-CDs, and histological analysis unveiled the biological mechanisms underlying the promotion of wound healing. This work explores the potential applications of CDs in the biomedical field, particularly in the context of promoting wound healing, thereby providing a theoretical foundation and practical reference for the use of carbon nanomaterials in biomedicine.
